moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with an unusual rook pawn move that immediately signals we're exploring the fringes of opening theory. The Kádas Opening is a rare system where we sacrifice central control early to create unusual imbalances and prepare quick kingside expansion. By moving h4 first, we're preparing g4 and potentially h5 to launch an aggressive kingside attack while our opponent is still developing—a strategy that works best when your opponent is unfamiliar with the resulting positions.

Black sensibly strikes at the center before we can establish a pawn on d4, claiming space and following opening principles. This central advance is natural and strong, preventing us from easily building a kingside attack since Black has gained time and space in the middle of the board.

We immediately challenge Black's e5 pawn with our central pawn, staying true to the Kádas Gambit plan—we're offering to sacrifice the d4 pawn to open lines for our pieces and create tactical complications. Notice we're not playing conservatively; by advancing d4 right away, we're saying we don't mind if Black captures, because we'll recapture with c3, gaining control of the center and opening lines for our queen and bishop.

Black logically captures our pawn, removing a central attacker and maintaining material equality. Black's d4 pawn is now somewhat advanced but also exposed, which will become important when we recapture with c3 next move.

We recapture on d4 with our c-pawn rather than moving the queen, which is the key move of the Kádas Gambit. This move supports the d4 pawn, controls critical central squares like b4 and d3, and prepares to develop our queen with tempo if we can attack Black's pieces. The c3 pawn also keeps our position flexible—we maintain control of d4 while we can still pursue our kingside attacking ideas with moves like g4, all while staying within a reasonable pawn structure.
